figures.caption: Fig. 1. The Nano-U segmentation model identifies traversable terrains (Right). It executes entirely on an ESP32-S3 microcontroller via the Rust-based MicroFlow engine, demonstrating feasibility for the tight memory and compute resources of a small mobile robot (Left).

Fig. 2. We train Nano-U via Quantization-Aware Distillation (QAD) where knowledge is distilled from a full-scale teacher while fake-quantization nodes simulate INT8 precision. The resulting model is compiled in Rust via MicroFlow for efficient execution on microcontrollers.

Fig. 3. Representative frames from the TinyAgri dataset: the Tomatoes sequence (top) features a clear vanishing point and a more uniform row structure, while the Crops sequence (bottom) presents denser vegetation and greater leaf overlaps.

Fig. 4. Qualitative comparison of segmentation masks. While Nano-U’s extreme parameter constraints prevent it from capturing the fine-grained edge details of the SAM2 ground truth, it reliably identifies the core traversable terrain.
